title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered at a repair café. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Grace, The Sewing Table Volunteer
Grace helped at the repair café on Saturday mornings by taking charge of torn everyday clothes and loose hems. She joined after seeing a box of uniforms from the local nursing home waiting to be thrown away. [47]Her years making costumes for amateur dance groups helped her repair awkward fabric quickly without making the mended area too stiff.[/47] At first she felt awkward asking people to describe how an item had been damaged, but those conversations often revealed why the clothing mattered. [48]She was most proud when she saved a child’s school blazer the night before a scholarship interview.[/48] The parents returned with homemade biscuits and a photograph. Grace learned to keep spare buttons sorted by colour in jam jars, because small details caused the longest delays. She says repair work has made her less impatient with old things; sometimes a frayed cuff simply needs someone to look closely.
texts.B:
### Marco, The Electrical Tester
Marco volunteered to test lamps, radios, kettles and small appliances brought in by neighbours, but only after the organisers agreed that safety checks would come before enthusiasm or politeness. [49]His first rule was that anything with a cracked plastic plug or exposed wire had to be refused until the owner bought replacement parts.[/49] Some visitors were visibly disappointed by this, so he practised explaining the danger without sounding superior. [50]His previous work as a school science technician helped him set up a clear testing station with labels, gloves and a notebook for results.[/50] He enjoyed patiently showing owners what had failed, especially when the fix was a simple fuse. The busiest session involved seven broken desk lamps, two hairdryers and one toaster that smoked dramatically. Marco says the café’s value is not just saving objects, but teaching people not to fear the inside of ordinary everyday machines at home.
texts.C:
### Nia, The Welcome Desk Organiser
Nia did not repair anything herself during sessions; she organised the welcome desk, which turned out to be the most crowded and anxious place in the hall. [51]She introduced numbered cards after two visitors argued about whose heavy coffee grinder had been placed on the table first.[/51] The system looked childish, but it immediately made the queue noticeably calmer. Nia’s background in event planning helped her notice where chairs, signs, spare forms and water jugs needed to go. [52]Her most uncomfortable task was telling people that volunteers could not promise a repair before the session ended.[/52] She hated disappointing them, especially elderly visitors who had carried heavy items by bus alone. Over time, after several difficult afternoons, she learned to offer realistic times and suggest nearby cafés for waiting nearby. Nia believes the welcome desk sets the emotional temperature of the event long before any volunteer screwdriver appears.
texts.D:
### Ethan, The Bicycle Corner Helper
Ethan ran a busy bicycle corner outside the repair café in the library car park after a neighbour persuaded him to bring his tools one Saturday. [53]He had expected punctures, but he was surprised by how many brakes had previously been adjusted so badly that the bikes were unsafe.[/53] He created a quick printed checklist before returning any bicycle to its owner. [54]The hardest repair involved freeing a rusted seat post while the owner explained that the bike was her only way to reach work.[/54] That pressure made Ethan breathe deeply and slow down rather than rush. He enjoyed teaching teenagers how to patch tubes, because they often left looking taller than when they arrived. [55]He now photographs unusual repairs and stores the images in a shared folder for future volunteers.[/55] [56]He hopes the bicycle corner will eventually run independently from the main indoor café during the busy summer months.[/56]

questions.question: used costume-making experience to mend difficult material without spoiling its feel?
questions.explanation: Grace used costume-making skills to repair awkward fabric without making it too stiff.

questions.question: was proud of fixing clothing needed for an important event the next day?
questions.explanation: Grace saved a child’s blazer the night before a scholarship interview.

questions.question: insisted that unsafe electrical items should not be worked on immediately?
questions.explanation: Marco refused items with cracked plugs or exposed wires until owners bought replacement parts.

questions.question: used science-lab experience to organise a safe testing area?
questions.explanation: Marco’s school science technician background helped him set up a labelled testing station.

questions.question: introduced a numbering system after a disagreement about order?
questions.explanation: Nia introduced numbered cards after two visitors argued about whose item came first.

questions.question: found it uncomfortable to manage expectations about whether work could be completed?
questions.explanation: Nia disliked telling people volunteers could not promise a repair before the session ended.

questions.question: was surprised by the number of dangerous brake problems?
questions.explanation: Ethan found many bicycle brakes had been badly adjusted and were unsafe.

questions.question: felt pressure during a repair because the owner depended on the item for work?
questions.explanation: Ethan repaired a rusted seat post while the owner explained the bicycle was her only way to reach work.

questions.question: keeps visual records of unusual repairs for other volunteers to consult?
questions.explanation: Ethan photographs unusual repairs and stores them in a shared folder.

questions.question: wants one part of the project to operate separately in the future?
questions.explanation: Ethan hopes the bicycle corner will run independently during summer.
